chapter: Chapter XII — Information to the Police and Their Powers to Investigate
text: An FIR (First Information Report) is the first step in a criminal case. Every information relating to the commission of a cognizable offence, if given orally to an officer in charge of a police station, shall be reduced to writing by him or under his direction, and be read over to the informant. Every such information, whether given in writing or reduced to writing, shall be signed by the person giving it, and the substance thereof shall be entered in a book called the FIR register. A copy of the information as recorded shall be given forthwith, FREE OF COST, to the informant. Police CANNOT refuse to register an FIR for a cognizable offence — this is mandatory under law. If police refuse, you can approach the Superintendent of Police or file a complaint before a Magistrate under Section 175 BNSS.

section: Section 175 — Magistrate can order FIR registration
text: If police refuse to register an FIR, any person may send the substance of the information, by post, to the Superintendent of Police. If the Superintendent of Police is satisfied that such information discloses the commission of a cognizable offence, he shall either investigate the case himself or direct an investigation to be made. Alternatively, you may present the complaint directly before a Magistrate who can direct registration of the FIR under Section 175 BNSS. Zero FIR: An FIR can be registered at ANY police station regardless of jurisdiction — called a Zero FIR — and then transferred to the appropriate station.

Under BNSS 2023, electronic FIR (e-FIR) can be filed online for certain offences through the state police portal or the National Cybercrime Reporting Portal (cybercrime.gov.in) for cyber offences. For cognizable offences, the FIR must be registered mandatorily. For non-cognizable offences, police only record information in the Daily Diary and forward it to a Magistrate. The informant has the right to receive a free copy of the FIR immediately upon registration.

Every person arrested has the following rights: (1) Right to be informed of the grounds of arrest immediately. (2) Right to inform a relative or friend about the arrest — police must allow this. (3) Right to consult and be defended by a legal practitioner of his choice from the moment of arrest. (4) Right to be produced before a Magistrate within 24 hours of arrest — police cannot hold you beyond 24 hours without Magistrate's order. (5) Right to bail in bailable offences. (6) Right to free legal aid if you cannot afford a lawyer. (7) Right to medical examination if claiming injury during arrest.

chapter: Chapter VII — Offences Against the State
text: Organised crime means any continuing unlawful activity including kidnapping, robbery, vehicle theft, extortion, land grabbing, trafficking of persons, manufacture and sale of illicit liquor, providing supari for murder, running prostitution rackets, cyber crimes, carried on by a person or persons forming an organised crime syndicate in pursuance of a common intention or conspiracy with a view to gain undue economic or other advantage. Punishment is imprisonment for not less than five years and may extend to imprisonment for life, along with fine.

chapter: Chapter V — Offences Against Women and Children
text: A man is said to commit rape who has sexual intercourse with a woman against her will, without her consent, with her consent obtained by putting her in fear of death or of hurt, with her consent when the man knows that he is not her husband, with or without her consent when she is under eighteen years of age. Punishment: rigorous imprisonment for a term not less than ten years but which may extend to imprisonment for life. Gang rape punishment is imprisonment for remainder of natural life.

section: Section 74 — Assault or criminal force to woman with intent to outrage her modesty
text: Whoever assaults or uses criminal force to any woman, intending to outrage or knowing it to be likely that he will thereby outrage her modesty, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to five years, and shall also be liable to fine. This section is commonly used in cases of molestation and eve-teasing.

Whoever, by words either spoken or intended to be read, or by signs or by visible representations, makes or publishes any imputation concerning any person intending to harm, or knowing or having reason to believe that such imputation will harm the reputation of such person, is said, to defame that person. It is not defamation to impute anything which is true concerning any person if it is for the public good. Truth is an absolute defence to defamation.
